slides.heading: The End of the Cold War and a New World Order
slides.lead: How the bipolar world of 1989 gave way to the collapse of the Soviet Union in 1991 - and to a globalised, single-superpower age.

image.alt: A cracked wall splitting a divided globe, symbolising the fall of the Iron Curtain.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='160' cy='90' r='66' fill='#8ba7c4'/><path d='M110 42 q22 18 4 44 q-18 22 6 52' fill='none' stroke='#2f5233' stroke-width='6'/><path d='M206 40 q-18 26 2 50 q16 22 -4 48' fill='none' stroke='#2f5233' stroke-width='6'/><path d='M160 20 L152 60 L168 92 L150 126 L164 160' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='7' stroke-linejoin='round'/><rect x='40' y='150' width='240' height='6' fill='#c8b89a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Open by recalling what learners already know about the Cold War from Topic 3: a decades-long rivalry between the capitalist West (led by the USA) and the communist East (led by the USSR), fought through proxy wars, an arms race and ideology rather than direct combat. Preview the two big questions of this topic: (1) Why did the Soviet bloc collapse so quickly between 1989 and 1991? (2) What kind of world order replaced it?

slides.heading: A bipolar world by the 1980s
slides.lead: For over forty years two superpowers divided the globe into rival camps.

blocks.text: After 1945 the world was **bipolar**: the United States led the capitalist, democratic West through **NATO**, while the Soviet Union led the communist East through the **Warsaw Pact**. Europe was split by what Churchill called an *Iron Curtain*, symbolised by the **Berlin Wall** (built 1961).

items: Competition took the form of an arms race, a space race and proxy wars (Korea, Vietnam, Angola).
Both blocs sought allies across Africa, Asia and Latin America.
The threat of nuclear war made direct superpower conflict too dangerous - hence a 'cold' war.

slides.notes: Reinforce the vocabulary learners will need: bipolar, superpower, NATO, Warsaw Pact, Iron Curtain, proxy war. Stress that by the 1980s this division seemed permanent - almost nobody predicted it would end within a decade. This makes the speed of the 1989-1991 collapse the central puzzle of the topic.

slides.heading: Why the Soviet system was under strain
slides.lead: By the mid-1980s deep structural weaknesses had built up inside the USSR.

blocks.heading: Stagnant economy
blocks.text: Central planning could not match Western living standards; there were shortages of goods and little innovation.

blocks.heading: Cost of empire
blocks.text: The arms race and the costly war in Afghanistan (1979-1989) drained the Soviet budget.

blocks.heading: Loss of belief
blocks.text: Corruption and censorship eroded public faith in communism across the USSR and Eastern Europe.

slides.notes: Frame these as long-term (structural) causes, to be distinguished from the short-term trigger of Gorbachev's reforms on the next slide. A strong FET analysis separates underlying causes from immediate triggers. Ask learners to consider: could reform alone have saved the system, or were these problems already fatal?

slides.heading: Gorbachev: glasnost and perestroika
slides.lead: The reforms meant to save the Soviet Union ended up hastening its collapse.

blocks.text: **Mikhail Gorbachev** became General Secretary of the Communist Party in March 1985. To modernise a failing system he launched two reforms: **glasnost** ('openness' - more free speech and criticism) and **perestroika** ('restructuring' - limited market reforms).

items: Glasnost allowed people to voice grievances the state had long silenced.
Perestroika loosened central control but disrupted the economy further.
Gorbachev also ended the Brezhnev Doctrine, signalling the USSR would no longer use force to prop up Eastern European communist governments.

image.alt: Two arrows, one opening outward and one rebuilding blocks, symbolising openness and restructuring.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='70' cy='80' r='34' fill='#8ba7c4'/><path d='M70 56 v48 M70 56 l-12 12 M70 56 l12 12' stroke='#f4efe6' stroke-width='6' fill='none' stroke-linecap='round'/><g fill='#2f5233'><rect x='140' y='96' width='22' height='22'/><rect x='166' y='96' width='22' height='22'/><rect x='153' y='72' width='22' height='22'/></g><rect x='134' y='120' width='68' height='6' fill='#c8b89a'/></svg>

slides.notes: The key analytical point: Gorbachev intended to REFORM communism, not to end it. By allowing criticism (glasnost) and abandoning the Brezhnev Doctrine, he removed both the fear and the force that had held the bloc together. This is a classic example of unintended consequences - useful for essay writing on causation.

slides.heading: Eastern Europe breaks free
slides.lead: Once Moscow would no longer intervene, communist governments fell one after another.

blocks.text: In 1989 a wave of largely peaceful revolutions swept Eastern Europe. **Poland** held partly free elections won by *Solidarity*; **Hungary** opened its border; and on **9 November 1989 the Berlin Wall fell**, its checkpoints overwhelmed by crowds.

items: Czechoslovakia's peaceful 'Velvet Revolution' ended communist rule.
In Romania the dictator Nicolae Ceausescu was overthrown and executed.
The images of ordinary people breaking down the Berlin Wall became the symbol of the Cold War's end.

image.alt: A section of a graffiti-covered wall with a large gap where bricks have been removed, people passing through.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 260 160' role='img'><rect width='260' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><g fill='#c8b89a' stroke='#b7a98d' stroke-width='1'><rect x='10' y='50' width='40' height='18'/><rect x='52' y='50' width='40' height='18'/><rect x='10' y='70' width='40' height='18'/><rect x='52' y='70' width='40' height='18'/><rect x='10' y='90' width='40' height='18'/><rect x='52' y='90' width='40' height='18'/><rect x='168' y='50' width='40' height='18'/><rect x='210' y='50' width='40' height='18'/><rect x='168' y='70' width='40' height='18'/><rect x='210' y='70' width='40' height='18'/><rect x='168' y='90' width='40' height='18'/><rect x='210' y='90' width='40' height='18'/></g><rect x='24' y='38' width='16' height='6' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='214' y='38' width='16' height='6' fill='#2f5233'/><g fill='#7a5a3a'><circle cx='118' cy='96' r='8'/><rect x='112' y='104' width='12' height='24'/><circle cx='142' cy='96' r='8'/><rect x='136' y='104' width='12' height='24'/></g><rect x='10' y='130' width='240' height='6' fill='#7a8b5a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Emphasise agency: these were largely peaceful, people-driven revolutions, not events dictated from above. Note the domino effect - once Moscow made clear it would not send in tanks (as it had in Hungary 1956 and Czechoslovakia 1968), each collapse encouraged the next. The fall of the Wall on 9 November 1989 is the single date most likely to appear in a source-based question. [SME] Confirm you wish to include the detail of Ceausescu's execution for this class; handle sensitively.

slides.heading: From the Wall to the end of the USSR, 1989-1991

blocks.alt: Timeline of key events from 1989 to 1991.

events.label: Solidarity wins partly free elections in Poland
Revolutions across Eastern Europe; Malta Summit
A hardline coup against Gorbachev fails
The Soviet Union is formally dissolved

slides.notes: Use this slide to consolidate the sequence before analysing consequences. Point out the accelerating pace: reforms from 1985, revolutions in 1989, and the USSR itself gone by the end of 1991 - only six years. Learners should be able to place at least the Wall (1989) and the dissolution of the USSR (1991) accurately.

slides.heading: The collapse of the Soviet Union
slides.lead: As the outer empire fell, the USSR itself broke apart into fifteen independent states.

blocks.text: Glasnost had also awakened **nationalism** inside the USSR. The Baltic states (Lithuania, Latvia, Estonia) and others demanded independence. In **August 1991** communist hardliners staged a coup against Gorbachev; it collapsed within days, largely discredited when crowds and **Boris Yeltsin** resisted it.

items: The failed coup shattered the authority of both the Communist Party and Gorbachev.
One by one the Soviet republics declared independence.
On 26 December 1991 the USSR was formally dissolved and Gorbachev resigned; the Cold War was over.

image.alt: A single large red star breaking into fifteen smaller separate stars.

slides.notes: Draw the distinction between the collapse of the outer bloc (Eastern Europe, 1989) and the disintegration of the USSR itself (1991). Nationalism unleashed by glasnost was decisive inside the union. Yeltsin's rise and Gorbachev's fall mark the transfer of power to a new Russia. Stress that this ended not just a government but a superpower and an entire ideological project.

slides.heading: One superpower, many questions
slides.lead: With the USSR gone, the United States stood as the world's only superpower - a 'unipolar moment'.

blocks.text: A new world order... freer from the threat of terror, stronger in the pursuit of justice, and more secure in the quest for peace.
blocks.cite: US President George H. W. Bush, address to Congress, 11 September 1990

blocks.text: Leaders spoke of a **new world order** in which nations would cooperate through the **United Nations** rather than divide into hostile blocs. Some thinkers even declared the triumph of liberal democracy and free markets as the model for the future.

items: The USA became the dominant military, economic and cultural power.
Old certainties disappeared and new conflicts (ethnic, regional) emerged.
Critics argued the 'new order' mostly served Western interests.

slides.notes: Introduce the concept of a unipolar world and the debate around it. The Bush quote is a genuine primary source useful for a source-based question. Mention Francis Fukuyama's 'end of history' thesis as the optimistic view, and contrast it with the pessimists who foresaw new instability (for example the Yugoslav wars of the 1990s). Encourage learners to weigh competing interpretations. [SME] Verify exact wording/date of the Bush quotation against the primary transcript before production.

slides.heading: The rise of globalisation
slides.lead: The end of the Cold War accelerated the integration of the world economy and culture.

blocks.heading: Economic
blocks.text: Freer trade, multinational companies and bodies like the WTO tied national economies together.

blocks.heading: Technological
blocks.text: The internet, satellite media and cheap communication shrank distances almost overnight.

blocks.heading: Cultural
blocks.text: Ideas, brands and popular culture spread worldwide, raising debates about identity and inequality.

slides.notes: Define globalisation as the growing interconnection of the world's economies, technologies and cultures. Stress that it did not begin in 1989 - but the collapse of the communist bloc removed a major barrier and opened former Eastern-bloc countries to the global market. Draw out both benefits (growth, communication) and criticisms (inequality, loss of local control), preparing learners for a balanced argument.

slides.heading: The Cold War's end and South Africa
slides.lead: The collapse of the Soviet bloc reshaped the politics of apartheid's final years.

blocks.text: The apartheid government had long justified repression as a defence against the *rooi gevaar* ('red danger') of communism. As the Cold War ended, this argument lost much of its force, and pressure for negotiation grew. On **2 February 1990** F. W. de Klerk unbanned the ANC, PAC and SACP, and on **11 February 1990** Nelson Mandela was released.

items: The Soviet Union had been an important backer of the ANC and SACP.
Its collapse weakened the ANC's external support but also removed the West's fear of a 'communist takeover' in South Africa.
Many historians see the end of the Cold War as one factor that helped make a negotiated settlement possible.

image.alt: Two hands meeting in a handshake over a map outline of South Africa.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M70 40 q40 -8 90 6 q22 30 6 66 q-40 26 -84 8 q-26 -34 -12 -80 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='70' y='84' width='44' height='16' rx='8' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect x='128' y='84' width='44' height='16' rx='8' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='108' y='82' width='26' height='20' rx='6' fill='#dcd3c2'/></svg>

slides.notes: This slide localises a world-history topic - essential for SAHO and for CAPS, which links the end of the Cold War to South Africa's transition. Make the causal claim carefully: the end of the Cold War was ONE of several factors (alongside internal resistance, sanctions and economic pressure) that shaped negotiations. Avoid overstating it as the single cause. [SME] The strength of the causal link between the Cold War's end and the 1990 unbannings is debated among historians - flag for SME to calibrate the wording for exam accuracy.

slides.heading: Why did the Cold War end? Two views
slides.lead: Historians disagree about what mattered most - a strong analysis weighs both.

columns.text: One view credits Western pressure: the arms race and firm leadership (Reagan, Thatcher) that the Soviet economy could not match, forcing it to give up.
Another view stresses internal decay: economic stagnation, Gorbachev's reforms and the choices of ordinary people in Eastern Europe brought the system down.

slides.notes: Model historiographical thinking: there is rarely a single cause. Encourage learners to synthesise - most historians combine both, seeing external pressure AND internal weakness. This kind of balanced, evidence-weighed judgement is exactly what the FET essay rubric rewards. Ask learners which view they find more convincing and why.

slides.heading: A changed world
slides.lead: The events of 1989-1991 reshaped politics, economics and daily life across the globe.

blocks.text: The end of the Cold War closed a defining chapter of the twentieth century. It ended the nuclear stand-off between the superpowers, opened former communist states to democracy and markets, and accelerated **globalisation** - but it did not bring the lasting peace many had hoped for.

items: New conflicts emerged, including ethnic wars in the former Yugoslavia.
The United States became the dominant world power, at least for a time.
Debates over inequality, migration and identity still shape today's globalised world.

slides.notes: Bring the topic up to the present ('1989 to present' in the CAPS heading). The 'new world order' proved more complicated and contested than early optimists predicted. This slide links back to the new-world-order and globalisation slides and sets up the review questions. Invite learners to connect these themes to current events they know.

slides.heading: What we learned - review questions
slides.lead: Use these CAPS-style questions to test causation, source analysis and extended writing.

blocks.text: **Summary:** Between 1989 and 1991 the Soviet bloc collapsed - driven by economic weakness, Gorbachev's reforms and popular revolutions - ending the Cold War. The result was a single-superpower, globalising world, and, in South Africa, part of the changing context of the transition to democracy.

blocks.label: Q1
blocks.question: Explain TWO reasons why the Soviet Union collapsed between 1989 and 1991.

blocks.label: Q2
blocks.question: Using the Berlin Wall as an example, explain why 1989 is seen as a turning point in world history.

blocks.label: Q3
blocks.question: 'The end of the Cold War helped make a negotiated settlement in South Africa possible.' Do you agree? Support your answer with evidence.

slides.notes: Use the three questions for written work or discussion; the mark allocations mirror CAPS short-answer, paragraph and extended-writing styles. Q3 is deliberately open so learners can argue either way, provided they use evidence - reward balanced judgement over a fixed 'correct' answer. Close by linking to the next section of work and to South Africa's own transition studied elsewhere in the syllabus.
